items.q: What is characteristic of Baroque music?
items.correct: Continuous bass line, ornamentation, terraced dynamics, and counterpoint.
items.why: Students project later styles backwards. Baroque (1600-1750) used basso continuo, harpsichord/organ, and terraced (sudden) dynamics. Piano hadn't yet replaced harpsichord.

items.q: What is a tone row in serial music?
items.correct: An ordered series of all 12 chromatic pitches, used as the basis of a piece.
items.misconception: A row of notes played together as a chord.
items.random_wrong: Any sequence of notes a composer chooses.
items.why: Students miss the SPECIFIC structure. Schoenberg's 12-tone technique uses ALL 12 pitches in a fixed order before any repeats, generating melodies and harmonies from that row.

items.q: How do you identify a major key from its key signature?
items.correct: The last sharp is the leading note (semitone below tonic); the second-to-last flat is the tonic.
items.misconception: The number of sharps or flats equals the tonic note number.
items.why: Students lack a reliable rule. For sharp keys: last sharp + 1 semitone = tonic (e.g., F sharp means G major). For flat keys: the second-to-last flat IS the tonic (or memorise: 1 flat = F major).
The answer examiners want is: Liverpool. “London” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A short repeated phrase is called a.
items.why: It's Riff. “Bridge” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Roughly which decade did rock and roll begin?
items.why: Exact answer: 1950. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
